prompt: Name one commonly-praised idea, trend, or practice in modern knowledge work that you think is overrated, and explain why.
response:
One commonly-praised idea in modern knowledge work that is arguably overrated is *hyper-productivity through constant optimization*—the belief that we should relentlessly refine our workflows, tools, and habits (e.g., time-blocking, inbox zero, task batching, productivity apps) to squeeze maximum output from every minute.

While efficiency has its place, this obsession often backfires. It can lead to *productivity theater*—spending more time managing tasks than doing meaningful work—and fosters a culture of busyness over impact. The constant tweaking of systems (e.g., switching between Notion, Todoist, or new time-tracking apps) becomes its own time sink. More importantly, it undervalues essential but less measurable aspects of knowledge work: reflection, creativity, serendipity, and rest.

True productivity isn’t about doing more things faster; it’s about doing the right things with clarity and sustainability. Yet the hyper-optimization trend often glorifies motion over progress, making it an overrated ideal that can actually undermine long-term effectiveness and well-being.
